packs.name: Warm
packs.purpose: Home feels good — pour into it.

tools.title: Close the Loop
tools.do: Write tomorrow's first step — one action only. Then stop.
tools.why: Your brain keeps running loops because it doesn't have a next handle. Give it one.

tools.title: Open Loops List (3-2-1)
tools.do: Write 3 things sitting open in your head. Mark 2 as 'can wait.' Pick 1 for tomorrow.
tools.why: The overwhelm isn't the work — it's not knowing which work. This makes the order obvious.

tools.title: Worry Container
tools.do: Write the worry down. Set a time tomorrow to actually deal with it — 10 minutes is enough.
tools.why: Your brain keeps the thought alive because it's afraid you'll forget it. Give it a slot and it lets go.

tools.title: If–Then Anchor
tools.do: Write this down: 'If I start spiraling, I write one next step and close.'
tools.why: Having a rule in place means you don't have to make a decision when you're already overwhelmed.

tools.title: The One Screen Rule
tools.do: Close every tab except the one that matters. Phone face down.
tools.why: Half the load isn't the work — it's the switching. Remove the switching.

tools.title: Two-Minute Brain Dump
tools.do: Set a timer for 2 minutes. Write everything in your head. Don't organise it — just get it out.
tools.why: Carrying it all in your head is expensive. Getting it out, even messy, is cheaper.

tools.title: Reframe the Urgency
tools.do: Ask yourself: will this actually matter in 72 hours? If no, mark it 'later' and mean it.
tools.why: Pressure makes everything feel urgent. Most of it isn't. This test cuts through it.

tools.title: Stop the Mental Rehearsal
tools.do: Say out loud: 'Not solving this now.' Then breathe out slowly, three times.
tools.why: The loop only keeps going because you haven't told it to stop. This does that.

tools.title: Tomorrow's Boundary Line
tools.do: Write one sentence: 'Tomorrow I start with ___ at ___.'
tools.why: Your brain needs to know where tomorrow begins before it can let today end.

tools.title: Priority Ladder
tools.do: Write one must-do, two should-dos, three could-dos. That's the ladder.
tools.why: When everything feels equal, nothing gets done. This makes the order obvious.

tools.title: Timebox Relief
tools.do: Decide: 'I'll give this 10 minutes tomorrow at ___.' Write it down.
tools.why: Your brain keeps working on it because it doesn't know when it'll get to. Give it a time.

tools.title: Cognitive Offload + Park It
tools.do: Write the problem in one line. Write one next action. Then leave it there.
tools.why: Abstract problems drain you. Named ones with a next step don't.

tools.title: Decision Freeze Breaker
tools.do: Pick the smallest step you could take that you could undo if it's wrong. Do that tomorrow.
tools.why: You're not frozen because you don't know what to do. You're frozen because you're trying to be certain. Start small.

tools.title: Good Enough Standard
tools.do: Write the minimum version of tomorrow's task that would count as done.
tools.why: If you don't define 'good enough,' your brain defaults to perfect. That's why it never ends.

tools.title: Physiological Sigh
tools.do: Inhale fully, then sneak in one more small inhale on top, then exhale slowly. Do it three times.
tools.why: This is the fastest way to tell your nervous system the threat is over. Takes under a minute.

tools.title: Extended Exhale
tools.do: Breathe in for 4 counts, out for 6. Six rounds.
tools.why: A longer exhale activates the part of your nervous system that says you're safe now.

tools.title: 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding
tools.do: Name 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear, 2 you can smell, 1 you can taste.
tools.why: Your brain can't be in a loop and in your senses at the same time. This forces the switch.

tools.title: Jaw-Shoulder Release
tools.do: Unclench your jaw, drop your shoulders, open your hands. Then breathe out slowly.
tools.why: You're probably holding tension you don't know about. Releasing it tells your body the day is over.

tools.title: Temperature Reset
tools.do: Splash cold water on your face or run it over your wrists. A cool cloth works too.
tools.why: Cold water on the face triggers a quick physiological reset. It actually works.

tools.title: Lights Down Switch
tools.do: Dim the lights. Put your phone somewhere that isn't next to your bed.
tools.why: Your environment is still signalling 'awake.' Change the signal.

tools.title: Body Scan
tools.do: Slowly move your attention down your body: forehead, jaw, chest, belly, hands, feet.
tools.why: When you've been in your head all day, your body disappears. This brings you back.

tools.title: One Song Reset
tools.do: Pick one song. Sit. No tasks while it plays.
tools.why: You need a gap between the day and the evening. One song is enough to create it.

tools.title: Close the Laptop Ritual
tools.do: Close the laptop and say out loud: 'Done for today.'
tools.why: The act of saying it makes it real. Your brain needs the cue to switch off.

tools.title: Brain Parking Lot
tools.do: Write the three thoughts keeping you awake. Label each one 'tomorrow.'
tools.why: They keep coming because your brain is afraid they'll get lost. Write them down and it stops.

tools.title: Micro-movement Reset
tools.do: Walk slowly around the room. Roll your shoulders. That's it.
tools.why: Stress leaves residue in your body. Even two minutes of movement discharges it.

tools.title: Longer Blink
tools.do: Soften your eyes and do 10 slow blinks like you're trying to come down a gear, not stay sharp.
tools.why: Visual intensity keeps your system activated. Slowing the eyes is a fast way to tell your body it can reduce vigilance.

tools.title: Sensory Anchor
tools.do: Hold something warm — a mug, a cup. Focus on the warmth in your hands and your breath.
tools.why: Warmth and breath together are two of the most reliable ways to bring your nervous system down.

tools.title: Sleep Protect Line
tools.do: Say: 'I'll think about this tomorrow.' Say it once more if you need to.
tools.why: Night thoughts feel urgent because they have no competition. Naming them out loud defers them.

tools.title: 5-Minute Nearness
tools.do: Go sit near them. No phone. Not there to fix anything — just there.
tools.why: Presence without agenda is one of the most effective things you can do when you're drained.

tools.title: Name the State, Not the Person
tools.do: Say it out loud: 'I'm overloaded, not upset with you.'
tools.why: When you go quiet, the people who love you fill in the gap with something worse. This stops that.

tools.title: One Warm Question
tools.do: Ask one question that has nothing to do with plans, schedules, or tasks.
tools.why: When home is all logistics, connection fades without anyone noticing. One real question breaks the pattern.

tools.title: Micro-appreciation
tools.do: Say one specific thing you appreciate about them. Out loud, to their face.
tools.why: Small warmth deposits matter more than big gestures. This is one.

tools.title: Phone Boundary
tools.do: Put your phone in another room for the next 10 minutes.
tools.why: Even if you don't look at it, the phone on the table changes the room. Remove it.

tools.title: Kids: 2-Minute Attention Burst
tools.do: Let them lead for 2 minutes — whatever they want to show you or play. No multitasking.
tools.why: Kids don't need hours. They need undivided. Two minutes fully present beats an evening half there.

tools.title: I'm Here Now Re-entry
tools.do: Say: 'I wasn't fully here earlier. I'm here now.'
tools.why: You don't need a long conversation. Just a clean acknowledgement that you're back.

tools.title: Reduce Tone Friction
tools.do: Deliberately slow down how fast you're talking and lower your volume.
tools.why: Your tone is carrying the stress even when your words don't. Changing it changes the room.

tools.title: One Deliberate Look
tools.do: Look at them for one full second before you speak or move on. Let your attention land visibly.
tools.why: People can feel when your attention actually arrives. One deliberate second changes the interaction.

tools.title: Logistics Container
tools.do: Say: 'Can we do the logistics stuff at ___? I want this time to actually connect.'
tools.why: Logistics expand to fill all available time unless you contain them. This does that.

tools.title: Guilt Flip
tools.do: Swap 'I'm failing at this' for 'I'm adjusting to a hard season.'
tools.why: Guilt makes you avoid the people you feel guilty about. Reframing it helps you stay.

tools.title: One Touch Anchor
tools.do: A hand squeeze, a brief hug, a shoulder touch — if it's welcome.
tools.why: When words are hard, physical contact is often the shortest path back.

tools.title: Transition Sentence
tools.do: Say: 'Give me 10 minutes to land, then I'm fully here.'
tools.why: You need a buffer. They need to know you're coming. This covers both.

tools.title: Tiny Repair Token
tools.do: Say it simply: 'I got snappy earlier. I'm sorry.'
tools.why: Small repairs done quickly are worth more than big repairs done later. This is enough.

tools.title: Return Time Promise
tools.do: Say: 'I need a bit of space. I'll come back at ___.' Then actually come back.
tools.why: Space is fine. Disappearing without a word isn't. The time promise is what makes it okay.

tools.title: Clean Repair
tools.do: Three sentences: 'I wasn't fully here. I care about you. I'm back now.'
tools.why: You don't need a full debrief to repair. Three honest sentences are enough.

tools.title: One-Topic Rule
tools.do: Say: 'Can we do one topic and then stop?' Then actually stop when it's done.
tools.why: Multiple topics when things are already tense almost always makes it worse.

tools.title: Soft Start
tools.do: Start with what you feel or need. Not with what they always do.
tools.why: The first sentence sets the tone. 'You always' guarantees defensiveness. 'I feel' keeps it open.

tools.title: No-Fixing Window
tools.do: Ask: 'Can we just be close for 10 minutes without fixing anything?'
tools.why: Sometimes connection is more useful than resolution. This creates a window for it.

tools.title: Misread Correction
tools.do: Say: 'I'm quiet because I'm overloaded — not because I don't care.'
tools.why: Silence reads as rejection when it's actually just exhaustion. Naming the difference stops the spiral.

tools.title: Repair Question
tools.do: Ask: 'What would actually feel supportive to you right now?'
tools.why: You can't always guess what they need. Asking directly skips the guesswork.

tools.title: Apology + Specific Change
tools.do: Say: 'I'm sorry I ___. Next time I'll ___.'
tools.why: An apology without a change is just words. The second sentence is what makes it real.

tools.title: Pause + Reset
tools.do: Stop. Breathe. Notice what's actually happening. Then proceed.
tools.why: The thing you say in the first 5 seconds of a reactive moment usually makes everything worse. This creates a gap.

tools.title: Tiny Closure
tools.do: Say: 'We're okay. We'll pick this up tomorrow when we're not running on empty.'
tools.why: Not everything needs to be resolved tonight. This gives both of you permission to stop.

tools.title: The Olive Branch
tools.do: Say it simply: 'I know it's been off between us. I don't want that.'
tools.why: You don't need to know whose fault it is to take the first step. This opens the door.

tools.title: Lower the Shoulders First
tools.do: Before you try to repair, drop your shoulders, unclench your jaw, and exhale once slowly. Then speak.
tools.why: Repair lands differently when your body is not still broadcasting threat. Soften the body before the words.

tools.title: Tomorrow Promise
tools.do: Say: 'I can't do this justice tonight. Can we talk tomorrow at ___?'
tools.why: Forcing a hard conversation when you're drained almost always makes it worse. This defers without dismissing.

tools.title: Reconnect Without Words
tools.do: Sit close. No agenda. No phone. Just be in the same space.
tools.why: When words aren't available, proximity is. This is enough.

tools.title: Reality Check
tools.do: Ask yourself: what do I actually know right now, and what am I filling in with guesses?
tools.why: The spiral is almost always built on guesses dressed up as facts. This separates them.

tools.title: One Clean Ask
tools.do: Ask them: 'Can you give me one sentence of reassurance?' That's it.
tools.why: You need something, but a big conversation might make things worse. One sentence is enough.

tools.title: 90-Second Rule
tools.do: Before you text or ask again, wait 90 seconds. The urge usually passes.
tools.why: The urge to reach out when anxious peaks and then falls. You just have to outlast the peak.

tools.title: Anchor Sentence
tools.do: Say to yourself: 'We're stressed, not unsafe.'
tools.why: Anxiety makes stress feel like danger. This is the reminder that it isn't.

tools.title: Reduce Checking
tools.do: Put the phone down for 5 minutes. Not silent — actually away from you.
tools.why: Checking makes the anxiety worse, not better. Every check is a vote that something is wrong.

tools.title: Self-Reassurance
tools.do: Say: 'I can sit with this uncertainty for 10 minutes.' Then sit with it.
tools.why: Anxiety shrinks when you stop running from it. Ten minutes of tolerating it is a win.

tools.title: Repair Without Chase
tools.do: Send: 'I miss you. No pressure — just wanted you to know. Whenever you can.'
tools.why: You can reach out without creating pressure. This is how.

tools.title: Draft Don't Deliver
tools.do: Write the message exactly as you want to send it. Save it. Do not send it for two minutes.
tools.why: Anxiety wants discharge, not accuracy. Drafting gives the discharge without making the moment bigger.

tools.title: What's Actually True Right Now
tools.do: Say: 'Right now, in this moment, I am safe.'
tools.why: Anxiety lives in what might happen. This sentence pulls you back to what's actually happening.

tools.title: Separate the Story from the Signal
tools.do: Say: 'The feeling is real. The story I'm telling about it might not be.'
tools.why: You can trust the feeling without trusting the story you're building around it.

tools.title: One Grounding Object
tools.do: Pick one object near you. Describe it in detail in your head — shape, texture, colour, weight.
tools.why: Your brain can't spiral and observe at the same time. Give it something concrete to focus on.

tools.title: The Enough Check
tools.do: Ask: 'Have I done enough today to be okay?' Most days, the answer is yes.
tools.why: You keep going because you haven't told yourself you're allowed to stop. This does that.

tools.title: Delay the Interpretation
tools.do: Say: 'I'll know more tomorrow. I don't have to decide what this means tonight.'
tools.why: The meaning you assign at 11pm is almost never the meaning you'd assign at 9am. Wait.

tools.title: Steady Breath Anchor
tools.do: Breathe in for 4, hold for 2, breathe out for 6. Four rounds.
tools.why: This specific rhythm — longer exhale than inhale — is one of the most reliable ways to reduce anxious activation.

tools.title: Space With Return Time
tools.do: Say: 'I need 20 minutes. I'll come back at ___.'
tools.why: Going quiet without a word triggers fear. The return time removes it.

tools.title: Name the State
tools.do: Say: 'I'm overloaded right now — not done with you.'
tools.why: Withdrawal looks like rejection until you name it. One sentence changes how it lands.

tools.title: Low-Pressure Connection
tools.do: Sit near them. No talking required. Just be in the room.
tools.why: Being near someone without the pressure to talk is often the easiest bridge back.

tools.title: One Sentence Truth
tools.do: Say: 'I care about you. I just can't talk yet.'
tools.why: They need to know you're not gone. This says that without requiring anything more.

tools.title: Re-entry Script
tools.do: Say: 'I'm back. I can do one small thing together.'
tools.why: Re-entry doesn't need a speech. A small normal thing done together is enough.

tools.title: Boundary Without Coldness
tools.do: Say: 'Not right now — but yes, later. I mean that.'
tools.why: 'No' without a 'yes later' reads as rejection. The second half is what keeps it warm.

tools.title: The Honest Signal
tools.do: Say: 'I'm here. I just need quiet right now.'
tools.why: Ambiguity is where anxiety grows. One clear sentence removes it.

tools.title: Small Presence Act
tools.do: Make tea or coffee for both of you. No words needed.
tools.why: When words are depleted, small acts carry the signal. This one says 'I'm still thinking of you.'

tools.title: The Check-In Text
tools.do: Send: 'I'm okay. Just processing. Talk later.'
tools.why: Silence from you becomes a story in their head. This text keeps the story accurate.

tools.title: Permission to Be Quiet
tools.do: Say: 'I'm not being cold — I just recharge in silence. It's not about you.'
tools.why: Your quiet is normal for you. It's not normal for them. Context stops the misread.

tools.title: Exit With Warmth
tools.do: Before you take space, squeeze their hand or give a brief touch.
tools.why: How you leave matters as much as the leaving. A touch changes the whole read.

tools.title: The Reset Walk
tools.do: Walk alone. No phone, no podcast. Just you and the walk.
tools.why: You need to discharge it, not drown it out. Walking without input does both.

tools.title: Leave the Door Open
tools.do: Before taking space, say one warm line that keeps the connection open: 'I'm taking a minute, not leaving you.'
tools.why: Space lands better when the bond stays visible. One soft signal prevents withdrawal from reading like rejection.

tools.title: This Isn't About You
tools.do: Say: 'This is work stress, not us. I just need to decompress for a bit.'
tools.why: Work follows you home in your body, your tone, your silence. This sentence makes sure it doesn't get read as something else.

tools.title: Lock the win
tools.do: Text or tell someone one thing that went well today. Say it out loud first.
tools.why: Anchors momentum while your state is clear and light.

tools.title: The idea you keep postponing
tools.do: You know the one. Write three sentences on it. Just three.
tools.why: Starts the work without triggering perfectionism.

tools.title: Say the thing
tools.do: There's a conversation you've been half-avoiding. Send the first sentence now.
tools.why: Uses clarity to reduce future drag.

tools.title: Make tomorrow stupid easy
tools.do: Write one sentence: what is tomorrow's first move the moment you sit down?
tools.why: Preserves clarity so tomorrow starts clean.

tools.title: The ask you haven't made
tools.do: What do you actually need from someone this week? Ask for it while you have the confidence.
tools.why: Prevents silent load and keeps pressure from building.

tools.title: Name what's actually working
tools.do: Not gratitude journaling — diagnosis. What conditions made today good? Write them down so you can repeat them.
tools.why: Turns a good day into repeatable conditions.

tools.title: The decision you've been circling
tools.do: You already know the answer. Write it down and commit to it in one line.
tools.why: Clear states are when decisions stick and reduce noise.

tools.title: Say it before it's obvious
tools.do: Tell your partner or kid one specific thing you love about them. Not general — specific.
tools.why: Specificity creates real warmth deposits.

tools.title: Make a tiny plan together
tools.do: "Want to do X this weekend?" Easy ask. Big warmth signal.
tools.why: Signals future togetherness with low effort.

tools.title: Actually be curious
tools.do: Ask one question you genuinely don't know the answer to. Let them talk.
tools.why: Restores connection beyond logistics.

tools.title: Touch base without agenda
tools.do: Sit near someone you love. No phone. Just existing in the same space intentionally.
tools.why: Quiet proximity builds safety and closeness.

tools.title: Start a small thing
tools.do: Light a candle, put music on, make something warm to drink — for both of you.
tools.why: Creates a shared environment shift toward home.

tools.title: The compliment you thought but didn't say
tools.do: You had a positive thought about someone today. Tell them. Takes 10 seconds.
tools.why: Turns internal warmth into real connection.

tools.title: Play something stupid
tools.do: A game, a bit, a joke. No purpose. Just lightness while it's available.
tools.why: Play strengthens bond without effort.

tools.title: What would feel good to finish?
tools.do: Not what's urgent. What would feel genuinely satisfying to complete this week?
tools.why: Directs rare capacity toward meaning, not just urgency.

tools.title: Write it down before it fades
tools.do: What's working right now — in work, at home, in yourself? One sentence each.
tools.why: Captures clarity so you can repeat it.

tools.title: Do the nice thing
tools.do: The small gesture you thought of but didn't act on. Do it now while the impulse is real.
tools.why: Turns good intent into real-world warmth.

tools.title: Protect tomorrow morning
tools.do: Decide one thing you won't let into the first hour tomorrow. Guard the window.
tools.why: Protects momentum before pressure enters.

tools.title: Tell someone they matter
tools.do: Not a partner — a friend, a collaborator, a parent. The one you keep meaning to reach. One message.
tools.why: Strengthens your support network before you need it.

tools.title: Use the energy on the right thing
tools.do: You have capacity right now that pressure usually steals. What's the one thing that deserves it today?
tools.why: Focuses rare capacity on what matters most.

tools.title: Tone check
tools.do: Drop your shoulders. Unclench jaw. Long exhale for 8 seconds. Repeat twice.
tools.why: Stops clear energy from turning into wired energy.

tools.title: Micro celebration
tools.do: Pick a tiny reward now: one song, a short walk, or a hot shower. Do it fully.
tools.why: Reinforces recovery without needing a crash later.

tools.title: Save one trigger, save one protector
tools.do: Write two bullets: what created this good state today, and what protected it.
tools.why: Makes the state repeatable without overthinking.

tools.title: Close one loose end
tools.do: Pick one small open loop (a reply, a note, a task). Do the first 30 seconds of it now.
tools.why: Prevents tomorrow from inheriting unnecessary friction.

tools.title: Two wins, one lever
tools.do: Name two wins from today and one lever you should pull again tomorrow.
tools.why: Creates intentional momentum, not accidental productivity.

tools.title: Arrival buffer
tools.do: Before you enter home mode: 2 minutes, no phone, no input. Just breathe and arrive.
tools.why: Prevents spillover and keeps warmth available.

tools.title: Tonight's guardrail
tools.do: Choose one thing you will not add tonight (work, debate, scrolling, heavy topic). Write it down.
tools.why: Protects the light state from being drained by one bad choice.

tools.title: Remove One Future Problem
tools.do: Use the good state to remove one small future friction point now.
tools.why: Light states are valuable because they let you make tomorrow easier before pressure returns.

tools.title: Think Out Loud
tools.do: Ask your partner to just listen for 3 minutes while you talk through what's clogging your head. No input needed from them — just air it.
tools.why: Externalising the load to another person works faster than writing it alone. They don't need to fix it.

tools.title: Visible Off Switch
tools.do: Close the laptop in front of them. Say out loud: 'I'm done now.' It sounds small. It works.
tools.why: The people around you need to see the transition, not just assume it happened. This makes it real for everyone.

tools.title: Floor Reset
tools.do: Get down on the floor with the kids for 2 minutes. Follow their lead. Don't multitask.
tools.why: The physical position change and the shift to their world actually breaks the mental loop faster than trying to think it away.

tools.title: Text the Open Loop
tools.do: Send your partner one message with what's still running in your head. One sentence. Not to solve it — just to externalise it.
tools.why: Your brain keeps it alive because it's afraid you'll lose it. Sending it transfers the load.

tools.title: Walk and Talk
tools.do: Ask your partner to walk around the block with you. Talk if you want. The movement alone is doing the work.
tools.why: Walking side by side is easier than face-to-face when you're still decompressing. The motion helps discharge it.

tools.title: Kids as Context Switch
tools.do: Ask your kid a question you genuinely don't know the answer to. Let them talk. Actually listen.
tools.why: Genuine curiosity about something outside your head is one of the cleanest ways to break the cognitive loop.

tools.title: Long Distance Load Check
tools.do: Voice note to your partner: 60 seconds, just what's in your head. You don't have to send it — but try.
tools.why: Talking through the load, even to a recording, offloads more than writing. Sending it keeps you connected.

tools.title: Weekly Full Clear
tools.do: 15 minutes, notebook only. Write everything open — work, home, unresolved, avoided. Then circle the one that needs to move first.
tools.why: Trying to manage everything in your head is the reason the load feels unbearable. Get it all out at once.

tools.title: The Shared Whiteboard
tools.do: Sit with your partner and get the shared logistics — bills, plans, tasks — out of your head and onto paper or a shared list. All of it.
tools.why: Shared mental load is double the weight. Getting it out of both your heads and into one place halves the pressure.

tools.title: Role Switch Signal
tools.do: Change out of work clothes the moment you're done. If you work from home, walk outside and back in.
tools.why: Your environment and your clothing are cues your brain uses to stay in work mode. Change the cue.

tools.title: Monthly Mind Map
tools.do: 30 minutes, pen and paper. What's unresolved at work? At home? In your relationship? What's the one move that would create the most relief?
tools.why: Monthly clarity is the difference between managing the chaos and being managed by it.

tools.title: Hand Them the Phone
tools.do: Give your phone to your partner to hold for 20 minutes. Not silent — physically away from you.
tools.why: Externalising the temptation removes the decision entirely. You can't check what you can't reach.

tools.title: Bedtime as Your Ritual
tools.do: Do the kids' bedtime slowly. Their pace, not yours. Read with them, sit in the quiet. Let it decompress you too.
tools.why: Kids' bedtime is one of the most underrated wind-down rituals for adults. The slowness is the point.

tools.title: Wind Down Call
tools.do: Short call with your partner — not to solve anything. 5 minutes. Just to hear their voice before you sleep.
tools.why: The vagus nerve responds to familiar voices. It's one of the fastest ways to downregulate from a distance.

tools.title: No Work Topics Block
tools.do: Tell your partner: 'No work topics for the next 20 minutes. I need to actually land.' Then keep it.
tools.why: Without a boundary, work conversations bleed into evenings indefinitely. This closes the window.

tools.title: Handoff and Out
tools.do: Tell whoever's around: 'I need 10 minutes to decompress. Then I'm fully here.' Then actually take the 10 minutes.
tools.why: You can't be present when you haven't arrived yet. The 10 minutes isn't selfish — it's preparation.

tools.title: End-of-Day Message
tools.do: Send your partner one line: what you're leaving at work tonight, and that you're done. Makes the close real across the distance.
tools.why: When they're not with you, the day doesn't close on its own. One message creates the boundary.

tools.title: Long Exhale Evening
tools.do: 15 minutes together: dim lights, no screens, just sit. Read your own thing or do nothing. No agenda, no conversation required.
tools.why: Shared stillness tells both your nervous systems the threat is over. It doesn't need to be interactive.

tools.title: Kids Quiet Hours
tools.do: Tell the kids: 'This is quiet hour. You can play but the volume stays low.' Then actually use it.
tools.why: Low-level noise is still stimulation. Quieting the environment is the first step to quieting your system.

tools.title: No Input Walk
tools.do: 10 minutes outside, no audio, no phone. Just walk. Let your thoughts unspool without directing them.
tools.why: Your nervous system needs input-free time to discharge. Walking without a podcast or music is the version that actually works.

tools.title: Parallel Downtime
tools.do: Both of you, same room, different things. Reading, phone-free screens, notebooks. No talking required. Just share the quiet.
tools.why: You don't need to debrief to decompress together. Proximity without pressure is its own kind of rest.

tools.title: Long Distance End Ritual
tools.do: Same time every evening, a short video call or voice note — just to mark the end of the day together. 5 minutes maximum.
tools.why: Distance makes evenings ambiguous. A small consistent ritual creates the close your nervous system needs.

tools.title: Send the Real Update
tools.do: Not 'I'm fine.' What's actually going on with you this week — one honest paragraph to your partner.
tools.why: Distance makes it easy to perform okay-ness. This keeps the real connection alive.

tools.title: Call Without a Reason
tools.do: Not to catch up or solve anything. Just to see their face for 5 minutes. No agenda.
tools.why: Most calls have purpose. This one just says: I wanted to see you. That's its own message.

tools.title: The Kid Interview
tools.do: Ask them five questions about their life this week — friends, school, what they noticed. Listen like you'd listen to someone you admire.
tools.why: Kids feel seen when adults treat their world as genuinely interesting. This is full presence, not polite attention.

tools.title: I'm Thinking of You
tools.do: One text. Not 'miss you' — something specific: a thing you noticed today that made you think of them.
tools.why: Specificity is the signal. Anyone can say miss you. A specific thought says you're actually present in their absence.

tools.title: Phone-Free Meal
tools.do: Both phones in a drawer for the whole meal. No exceptions, no quick checks.
tools.why: The phone on the table changes what kind of attention is possible. Removing it changes the whole meal.

tools.title: Story Mode
tools.do: Ask your kid to tell you about something that happened today. Don't guide it, don't speed it up. Just receive.
tools.why: You don't need energy to receive. Listening is one of the few present-moment actions that works when you're drained.

tools.title: Future Cast
tools.do: Send a message about something specific and real you're looking forward to doing together. Not abstract — a specific plan.
tools.why: Future plans create felt closeness even across distance. This is the easiest warmth deposit from far away.

tools.title: Full Evening In
tools.do: No screens after 8pm. Read, talk, play something together. Make it the default one evening a week.
tools.why: You can't build depth in 5-minute windows. One real evening creates more closeness than seven half-present ones.

tools.title: Distance Repair
tools.do: One message: 'I've been off lately. It's not you — I'm navigating something hard. I wanted you to know.'
tools.why: When you go quiet from a distance, the story they fill in is usually worse than what's true. This corrects it.

tools.title: Apology to the Kids
tools.do: Get on their level and say simply: 'I was grumpy earlier. That wasn't about you. I love you.'
tools.why: Kids absorb your emotional state and make it about themselves. This corrects the record quickly.

tools.title: Voice Note Repair
tools.do: Record a voice message — not a text. 60 seconds. Tell them what's been going on and that you're coming back.
tools.why: Voice carries tone that text can't. When repairs are needed across distance, your voice does more than your words.

tools.title: Re-entry With Kids
tools.do: Get on their level. Tell them you're back. Ask one question. Actually wait for the answer.
tools.why: Re-entry doesn't need a ceremony. Getting physically low and being curious is enough to close the gap.

tools.title: The Honest Catch-Up
tools.do: Not a logistics call. Tell your partner one real thing about how the week has actually felt — not how it went.
tools.why: Status updates maintain connection. Honest feeling updates build it. These are different conversations.

tools.title: Name It for the Kids
tools.do: Tell them directly: 'I've been stressed from work this week, not from you. You haven't done anything wrong.'
tools.why: Kids don't have the context to know the stress isn't about them. Saying it out loud removes the fear.

tools.title: The Proper Conversation
tools.do: No phones, no distractions. Pick one topic you've been avoiding. Set a 15-minute limit. Stay in it.
tools.why: Avoided conversations compound. One honest 15-minute conversation is worth a month of tiptoeing around it.

tools.title: Stop Disappearing
tools.do: If you've gone quiet for a few days, one message: 'I've been in my head. I'm coming back. Not pulling away.'
tools.why: Silence reads as distance unless you name it. One sentence corrects the story before it calcifies.

tools.title: Kids as Anchor
tools.do: When the spiral starts, shift your full attention to what your kid is doing right now. Just observe for 60 seconds.
tools.why: Your brain cannot spiral and be present at the same time. Kids are one of the most effective anchors to the present moment.

tools.title: Say the Spiral Out Loud
tools.do: Tell your partner: 'I'm spiraling about ___.' Just naming it, out loud to them, is the first step out.
tools.why: Spirals are often just overloaded internal processing. Getting it out to another person ends it faster than thinking it alone.

tools.title: Write Before You Send
tools.do: Write the anxious message in full. Read it back. Then decide whether sending it is actually what you need — or just what you want to do right now.
tools.why: Messages written from the peak of anxiety almost always escalate things. The gap between writing and sending is where clarity lives.

tools.title: Physical Break with Kids
tools.do: Pick them up, chase them around the room, do something physical together for 2 minutes. The body snap breaks the thought loop.
tools.why: Physical play with kids requires presence. It's impossible to spiral and chase a 4-year-old at the same time.

tools.title: Externalise It Together
tools.do: Ask your partner to just listen while you get it out — 10 minutes, no advice needed. Then stop.
tools.why: Spirals are often just overloaded internal processing. Getting it out to another person often ends it faster than thinking it through alone.

tools.title: One Truthful Line
tools.do: Not the anxious version. Write one sentence that is simply true: what you're feeling, without the story around it. Send that.
tools.why: Anxiety inflates the story. The bare truth is almost always smaller and safer to share.

tools.title: Parent Handoff
tools.do: Tell the other parent or whoever is with you: 'I need 15 minutes.' Then use them without guilt.
tools.why: You cannot regulate yourself and manage children at the same time when flooded. The handoff is not abandoning — it's responsible.

tools.title: Low Pressure Call
tools.do: Message first: 'Can we talk for 5 minutes? No agenda — I just want to hear your voice.' Then call.
tools.why: No-agenda calls feel different to both of you. The lightness of no purpose is the point.

tools.title: Quiet Time Block
tools.do: Tell the kids you need quiet time. Set them up with something. Take 10 minutes — no phone, just quiet.
tools.why: Kids can handle a parent needing space if it's explained simply and they have something to do. This is better for everyone.

tools.title: The Witnessed Reset
tools.do: Take your space in the same house. Tell them where you are and when you'll be back. Don't disappear.
tools.why: Space inside the home is possible and valid. The difference between healthy space and withdrawal is one short sentence.

tools.title: Going Quiet Message
tools.do: Send: 'Going off for a bit to reset. Not pulling away — just need space. Back at ___.'
tools.why: Disappearing without a word is the thing that does damage. This one message prevents the misread.

tools.title: Delegate the Room
tools.do: Give one of the kids a real small job — 'can you be in charge of setting the table?' Use the 5 minutes you just created.
tools.why: Kids respond well to responsibility. You get a gap, they get agency. Both genuine.

tools.title: Come Back Smaller
tools.do: Re-enter with one small normal thing — make drinks, sit together, watch something brief. No big conversation needed.
tools.why: The re-entry doesn't need to be proportional to the absence. One small normal act closes the gap faster than a speech.

tools.title: Explain Your Quiet
tools.do: Say: 'I recharge in silence — it's not about you. I'll be back properly in ___.'
tools.why: What's normal for you isn't obvious to them. Context removes the fear. This takes 10 seconds.

tools.title: Share the Board
tools.do: Tell your partner one thing you're still carrying from work. Not to solve it — just to say it out loud to them.
tools.why: Shared verbal offload halves the weight faster than internal processing. They don't need to fix it.

tools.title: Do it Together
tools.do: Ask your partner to sit with you while you write your one next step. They don't help — just be there.
tools.why: Presence cuts the cognitive weight of difficult tasks. You don't need input from them — just company.

tools.title: Kids' Question Detour
tools.do: When a kid asks you something, answer it fully and slowly. Let that conversation be the interruption.
tools.why: Fully engaging with a child's question forces your prefrontal cortex out of work mode. It's a hard reset.

tools.title: Snack Stop Together
tools.do: Get a snack with your kid. Sit at the kitchen table. No screens, no planning. Just eat together for 5 minutes.
tools.why: Eating with someone else forces a natural pause. The social context breaks the loop better than forced stopping.

tools.title: Build the List Together
tools.do: Ask your partner to help you write the open loops — they often see what you're missing. 10 minutes, pen and paper.
tools.why: A second brain sees the gaps yours has become blind to. Two people catching your open loops is faster than one.

tools.title: Voice Note the Load
tools.do: Record a 60-second voice note to yourself — talk through what's still open. Then listen back once.
tools.why: Hearing yourself say the problem externalises it more than writing. Your brain registers the load as filed.

tools.title: Send the Status
tools.do: Text your partner one honest line about what's still running in your head tonight. Not to fix it — just to say it.
tools.why: Externalising the load helps. Sending it to them keeps the connection open while you're apart.

tools.title: Remote Planning Call
tools.do: 10-minute call: each of you says the one thing you most need to get out of your head this week. No solving — just naming.
tools.why: Two people naming their biggest loop cuts both loads simultaneously. Efficiency with connection.

tools.title: Async Brain Dump
tools.do: Voice note or long message: everything in your head, unfiltered. Send it to your partner. Don't wait for a reply.
tools.why: The act of sending transfers the weight. They don't need to respond — you just needed somewhere to put it.

tools.title: Shared Task List
tools.do: Write the shared to-do list with your partner over message — divide it, label it, leave it. Not urgent, just visible.
tools.why: Shared mental load is halved when it lives somewhere neither of you has to hold it in your heads.

tools.title: Wall Press Reset
tools.do: Stand facing a wall and press both palms into it for 20 seconds. Then step back and take one slower breath.
tools.why: Physical pressure gives the load somewhere to go. It interrupts cognitive spin through the body, not more thinking.

tools.title: Desk Shutdown
tools.do: Clear one visible work trace: close the notebook, stack the papers, or put the charger away. One physical shutdown move only.
tools.why: The brain follows visible cues. Removing one work cue helps the mind stop acting like work is still active.

tools.title: Slow Bedtime
tools.do: Do the bedtime routine slowly. No rushing them. Let their pace set yours tonight.
tools.why: Kids' bedtime is naturally slow. Surrendering to their rhythm instead of fighting it is a passive wind-down.

tools.title: Read to Them
tools.do: Read to your kid for 10 minutes. The act of reading out loud at a slow pace regulates your own breathing.
tools.why: Slow oral reading is one of the few parental tasks that activates your own parasympathetic system.

tools.title: Lay With Them
tools.do: After lights out, stay for 5 minutes. Don't talk. Just breathe. Let the room be quiet.
tools.why: The dark, quiet environment of a child's bedroom is one of the few places adults are permitted to simply stop. Use it.

tools.title: Kids' TV Rule
tools.do: Put on something they love. Sit with them. No phone. Just rest your brain in their show.
tools.why: Passive viewing with a child is one of the lowest-effort ways to stop thinking. Their engagement removes the need for yours.

tools.title: Good Night Ritual
tools.do: End the day with the same short ritual every night: one minute of slow breathing before sleep. Same time, same sequence.
tools.why: Rituals work because they're predictable. Your nervous system starts winding down before you've finished them.

tools.title: Wind Down Together Apart
tools.do: Same time, both of you: no screens, 10 minutes. You each do your own version. Text each other after.
tools.why: Synchronised wind-down across distance creates the felt connection of shared evening without the call pressure.

tools.title: Send the Worry
tools.do: Text your partner the thing keeping you awake tonight. Not to solve. Just to send it somewhere.
tools.why: Sending a worry transfers the cognitive custody. You're not alone with it anymore — even at a distance.

tools.title: Call Before Sleep
tools.do: A 5-minute call — no agenda, no catching up. Just say goodnight properly. Then actually sleep.
tools.why: The goodnight call anchors the end of the day. Without it, long-distance evenings stay open-ended and unsettled.

tools.title: One Song Each
tools.do: Send each other one song — the one that matches tonight. No explanation needed.
tools.why: Music is one of the fastest ways to feel understood. This is connection without effort.

tools.title: Solo Presence Practice
tools.do: Put the phone down. Sit in the room you're in. Notice five things about it that you've never paid attention to before.
tools.why: Presence is a skill, not a state. Practising it alone — without the pressure of others — is where it actually builds.

tools.title: Write What Matters Tonight
tools.do: Write one sentence: who most needs you to be present tonight and what that would actually look like.
tools.why: Presence requires intention. Without naming what it looks like, it defaults to whatever the phone or habit decides.

tools.title: Pre-Home Arrive First
tools.do: Five minutes in the car or outside before you go in. Breathe. Name what you're about to walk into. Then walk in.
tools.why: The quality of your arrival sets the tone for the whole evening. Five minutes outside is worth an hour of damage repair inside.

tools.title: Presence Audit
tools.do: Honest question to yourself: in the last 24 hours, who got your real attention? What does that tell you about tonight?
tools.why: You can't change your presence without first seeing what it actually looks like. This is that look.

tools.title: Guilt Into Action
tools.do: Name what you feel guilty about. Then write one specific thing you can do tonight to reduce it. Not fix it — reduce it.
tools.why: Guilt is only useful if it points somewhere. This converts it from background weight into a small next move.

tools.title: Phone Away Before Entry
tools.do: Phone goes in your bag or pocket before you open the front door. It stays there for the first 15 minutes.
tools.why: The first 15 minutes of re-entry sets the tone for the whole evening. The phone makes them worse every time.

tools.title: Decide Who You Want to Be Tonight
tools.do: One sentence before you arrive: 'Tonight I want to be ___.' Not perfect — just intentional.
tools.why: Presence is a choice made in advance. Without the choice, habit decides. This makes it conscious.

tools.title: Protect the Window
tools.do: Block the next 30 minutes. No calls, no replies, no work. Tell your team or turn on do not disturb before you arrive.
tools.why: Presence is impossible when you're on call. Protecting a window in advance means you actually get to be there.

tools.title: Share Your Day Video
tools.do: 30-second video or voice note of something from your day — not an update, something specific and real. Send it.
tools.why: Sharing small real moments across distance builds more closeness than catch-up calls. Specificity is the active ingredient.

tools.title: Ask What Their Day Felt Like
tools.do: Not 'how was your day.' Ask: 'What did today feel like for you?' Then just listen.
tools.why: Asking about feeling gets real answers. Asking about events gets summaries. The difference is the whole connection.

tools.title: Morning Check-In
tools.do: One message at the start of their day: 'Thinking of you today. Anything I should know?'
tools.why: Being present doesn't only mean evenings. A morning message signals you're in their corner before their day starts.

tools.title: Photo From Your Day
tools.do: Send one photo of something that made you think of them, or just of where you are right now.
tools.why: Photos make the distance visible and smaller at the same time. One image says 'I'm real and I'm thinking of you.'

tools.title: Make Presence Easier
tools.do: Pick the easiest form of presence available right now: sit nearby, ask one question, or offer one touch. Choose the lightest version and do that.
tools.why: Presence fails when the bar is too high. Lowering the bar makes connection more likely to actually happen.

tools.title: Set the Scene
tools.do: Change one thing in the room to make connection easier: lower the lights, put the phone away, sit down, or put on soft music.
tools.why: Connection is easier when the environment helps instead of fights it.

tools.title: Self Repair
tools.do: Write honestly: what made you hard to be around lately, and one thing you'll do differently tonight.
tools.why: Most repair happens outward. This one is inward — naming what's been off without someone to say it to.

tools.title: Prepare the Repair
tools.do: Before you see them tonight, write what you want to say in one sentence. Practise it out loud once.
tools.why: Repairs that haven't been thought through come out wrong. One sentence prepared in advance lands differently.

tools.title: Acknowledge to Yourself First
tools.do: Say out loud to yourself: 'I've been distant. I want to come back.' Say it before you're with anyone else.
tools.why: Internal acknowledgement precedes external repair. You can't offer what you haven't yet recognised.

tools.title: Write the Letter
tools.do: Write a message you won't send — everything you want to say but can't yet. Let it be messy and complete.
tools.why: Unsent letters are one of the most effective emotional processing tools. The act of writing it is the release.

tools.title: Decide the First Step Back
tools.do: Write one thing you'll do tonight that signals to them — without words — that you're back.
tools.why: Re-entry doesn't always need a conversation. Sometimes a physical act is clearer than a speech.

tools.title: Post-Rupture Reflection
tools.do: Write three questions: What happened? What was I carrying that made it worse? What do I want instead next time?
tools.why: Ruptures that aren't examined repeat. Ten minutes of honest reflection is what changes the pattern.

tools.title: Ask for More Time
tools.do: Text: 'I'm not ready to talk yet but I haven't gone anywhere. Can I have until tomorrow?'
tools.why: Asking for time is different from disappearing. This keeps the connection while you process solo.

tools.title: Be Back Soon
tools.do: Send one message: 'I know I've been quiet. I'm working through something. I'm coming back.'
tools.why: Three days of silence needs acknowledgement, not explanation. This stops the story they're building.

tools.title: After School Repair
tools.do: Find a quiet moment before dinner. Get low. Say simply: 'How are you actually doing? I want to know.'
tools.why: Kids store what adults don't ask about. Creating a specific moment for them to release it repairs the distance.

tools.title: Reconnect Through Play
tools.do: Pick a 5-minute game they love — simple, physical, or silly. Let them win. Let yourself be fully in it.
tools.why: Play is the fastest repair language with kids. It signals safety, warmth, and full attention without a single serious word.

tools.title: Video Repair
tools.do: Short video call — 5 minutes, no agenda, no debrief. Just be face-to-face for a moment.
tools.why: Text can't do what a face can. When things have been off, a brief video call repairs faster than 20 messages.

tools.title: Own It Directly
tools.do: Message: 'I've been hard to reach lately. That's on me. I want to change that.'
tools.why: Owning it without justification is rarer and more powerful than explanation. This is how trust rebuilds.

tools.title: Warm Exit
tools.do: If you need to pause the interaction, do it with warmth: 'I need a minute, and I care about this. I'm coming back.'
tools.why: A pause does less damage when care stays visible.

tools.title: Stay Visible First
tools.do: Before explaining anything, give one line that makes your care visible: 'I care about you and I don't want this to stay off.'
tools.why: People hear explanation differently once care has been made explicit.

tools.title: Write the Worst Case
tools.do: Write the worst version of what you're afraid is true. Then write the most likely version. Notice the gap.
tools.why: Spirals live in vague catastrophe. Writing the worst case makes it concrete and therefore smaller.

tools.title: Name What You're Actually Afraid Of
tools.do: Ask yourself: underneath the spiral, what am I actually afraid of? Write it plainly. Just that.
tools.why: Spirals are usually protecting a core fear. Naming the fear directly is faster than managing the spiral.

tools.title: The Two-Minute Rule
tools.do: Before any anxious action — the check, the text, the re-read — wait exactly 2 minutes. Then decide.
tools.why: Most anxious impulses peak in under 2 minutes. This gap is enough to let the urgency pass.

tools.title: Physical Anchor With Kids
tools.do: Hold your kid's hand, give a hug, or sit close. Physical contact with a child grounds you in the present moment.
tools.why: Touch anchors you to the present. A spiral requires you to be in the future or past. You can't be both at once.

tools.title: Regulated Play
tools.do: Do something physical with the kids: dance, chase, kick a ball. Your nervous system co-regulates with theirs.
tools.why: Children's nervous systems are naturally regulated during play. Being physically close to them in motion pulls yours toward the same state.

tools.title: Read a Story
tools.do: Read a children's book out loud. Slowly. Let their world replace your thoughts for 10 minutes.
tools.why: Story reading requires your attention without your analysis. It occupies the spiral-prone part of your brain without stimulating it.

tools.title: Routine as Regulator
tools.do: Do the next routine task with the kids — bath, dinner, homework. Let the structure contain the anxiety.
tools.why: Routine is one of the most underrated anxiety tools. The predictable sequence doesn't require decision-making, which is exactly what anxious brains need.

tools.title: Send a Signal, Not a Spiral
tools.do: If you need to reach out, send only the clean signal: what you feel or need in one sentence. No story, no interpretation.
tools.why: A signal reduces uncertainty. A spiral increases it.

tools.title: The Blank Hour
tools.do: Block one hour with no people, no input, no tasks. Just be in a room alone with no agenda.
tools.why: Avoidant nervous systems regulate in solitude. Not depression — recovery. This is the legitimate version.

tools.title: Name the Wall
tools.do: Say to yourself: 'I'm shutting down because I'm depleted — not because I don't care.' Say it clearly.
tools.why: Avoidant shutdown feels like not caring. It's usually depletion. The distinction changes what you do next.

tools.title: Solo Decompress Walk
tools.do: Walk alone for 15–20 minutes. No destination, no purpose, no phone. Just discharge.
tools.why: Avoidant patterns discharge through movement and solitude more than through stillness. This is the active version of rest.

tools.title: Write the Re-entry Plan
tools.do: Before you're back in contact: write one sentence about how you want to show up when you return.
tools.why: Re-entry after space often goes wrong because it hasn't been prepared. One sentence of intention changes the odds.

tools.title: Do One Thing You Enjoy
tools.do: One activity you do for yourself alone — read, cook, draw, run. No performance, no productivity. Just yours.
tools.why: Avoidant types often deny personal restoration. This isn't escape — it's maintenance. Without it, nothing is available for others.

tools.title: No Explanation Needed
tools.do: Remind yourself: you don't owe anyone a real-time account of your internal state. You're allowed to process privately.
tools.why: Avoidant types often feel guilty for needing space. This isn't a flaw — it's a legitimate nervous system pattern.

tools.title: Note Then Space
tools.do: Before you go quiet, send one message: 'Need some time. Not pulling away — will be back at ___.'
tools.why: Taking space from distance is higher risk than at home. The message converts withdrawal to bounded space.

tools.title: Short Video Instead of Call
tools.do: Record a 30-second video of where you are and what you're doing. Send it without requiring a response.
tools.why: Full presence from a distance isn't always possible. Small-window presence is better than complete absence.

tools.title: Ask for Low-Pressure Time
tools.do: Message: 'Can we talk tonight? Nothing heavy — I just want to hear your voice.'
tools.why: No-agenda connection is less threatening to avoidant patterns than repair conversations. This is the easier bridge back.

tools.title: Sit With the Discomfort
tools.do: Don't fix it, don't text, don't scroll. Just sit with whatever is unresolved for 5 minutes and let it be there.
tools.why: Avoidant patterns are often about escaping discomfort. Tolerating it for 5 minutes — without acting — is the first step to not being controlled by it.

tools.title: Sit Back, Not Forward
tools.do: Before responding, physically lean back or sit back in the chair instead of moving toward the tension.
tools.why: Avoidant systems often need a physical cue that says 'pause' before they can stay connected without fleeing.

tools.title: One Tiny Thing Side by Side
tools.do: Do one tiny normal thing near them without making it a conversation: fold something, make tea, straighten the room, sit down nearby.
tools.why: Avoidant re-entry is easier through side-by-side normality than face-to-face intensity.
